or **Srirangapattana** is a town and headquarters of one of the seven [Taluks](/wiki/Tehsil "Tehsil") of [Mandya district](/wiki/Mandya_district "Mandya district"), in the [Indian](/wiki/India "India") [State](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_India "States and territories of India") of [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"). It gets its name from the [Ranganathaswamy temple](/wiki/Ranganathaswamy_Temple,_Srirangapatna "Ranganathaswamy Temple, Srirangapatna") consecrated around 984 CE. Later, under the British rule, the city was renamed to Seringapatam. Located near the city of [Mandya](/wiki/Mandya "Mandya"), it is of religious, cultural and historic importance.

**Srirangapatna** or **Srirangapattana** is a town and headquarters of one of the seven [Taluks](/wiki/Tehsil "Tehsil") of [Mandya district](/wiki/Mandya_district "Mandya district"), in the [Indian](/wiki/India "India") [State](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_India "States and territories of India") of [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"). It gets its name from the [Ranganathaswamy temple](/wiki/Ranganathaswamy_Temple,_Srirangapatna "Ranganathaswamy Temple, Srirangapatna") consecrated around 984 CE. Later, under the British rule, the city was renamed to Seringapatam. Located near the city of [Mandya](/wiki/Mandya "Mandya"), it is of religious, cultural and historic importance.

The monuments on the island town of Srirangapatna have been nominated as a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") World Heritage Site, and the application is pending on the tentative list of UNESCO.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Srirangapatna has since time immemorial been an urban center and place of pilgrimage. During the [Vijayanagar empire](/wiki/Vijayanagar_empire "Vijayanagar empire"), it became the seat of a major viceroyalty, from where several nearby vassal states of the empire, such as Mysore and [Talakad](/wiki/Talakad "Talakad"), were overseen. When perceiving the decline of the Vijayanagar empire, the rulers of Mysore ventured to assert independence, Srirangapatna was their first target. [Raja Wodeyar I](/wiki/Raja_Wodeyar_I "Raja Wodeyar I") vanquished Rangaraya, the then viceroy of Srirangapatna, in 1610 and celebrated the *[Navaratri](/wiki/Navaratri "Navaratri")* festival in the town that year.[[3]](#cite_note-3) It came to be accepted in time that two things demonstrated control and signified sovereignty over the Kingdom of Mysore by any claimant to the throne, including the successful holding of the 10-day-long *Navaratri* festival, dedicated to [Chamundeshwari](/wiki/Chamundeshwari "Chamundeshwari"), patron goddess of Mysore and control of the fort of Srirangapatna, the fortification nearest to the capital city of Mysore.

Srirangapatna remained part of the Kingdom of Mysore from 1610 to after India's independence in 1947; as the fortress closest to the capital city of Mysore, it was the last bastion and defence of the kingdom in case of invasion.

### Hyder and Tipu

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Hyder and Tipu")]

Main articles: [Hyder Ali](/wiki/Hyder_Ali "Hyder Ali") and [Tipu Sultan](/wiki/Tipu_Sultan "Tipu Sultan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Hale_Sethuway_Street_on_Kaveri_River.jpg/250px-Hale_Sethuway_Street_on_Kaveri_River.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hale_Sethuway_Street_on_Kaveri_River.jpg)

Hale Sethuway Street

Srirangapatna became the capital of Mysore under [Hyder Ali](/wiki/Hyder_Ali "Hyder Ali") and [Tipu Sultan](/wiki/Tipu_Sultan "Tipu Sultan").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) When Tipu finally dispensed with the charade of deference to the Wodeyar [Maharaja](/wiki/Maharaja "Maharaja") who was actually his captive, and proclaimed the "Khudadad State" under his own kingship, though the then incumbent Wodeyar scion [Chamaraja Wodeyar IX](/wiki/Chamaraja_Wodeyar_IX "Chamaraja Wodeyar IX") was still officially the king of Mysore.

During Tipu Sultan's wars against the British, Kodavas, and Malabar rajas; he rounded up entire communities of [Nairs](/wiki/Nairs "Nairs"), [Kodavas](/wiki/Kodavas "Kodavas"), and [Mangalorean Catholics](/wiki/Mangalorean_Catholics "Mangalorean Catholics") in the conquered regions and deported them to Srirangapatna, where they were held in bondage until they received freedom from captivity as a result of Tipu's defeat by the British in 1799.

### Treaty of Seringapatam, 1792

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Treaty of Seringapatam, 1792")]

Main article: [Treaty of Seringapatam](/wiki/Treaty_of_Seringapatam "Treaty of Seringapatam")

The Treaty of Seringapatam (also called Srirangapatna), signed 18 March 1792, ended the Third Anglo-Mysore War. Its signatories included Lord Cornwallis on behalf of the British East India Company, representatives of the Nizam of Hyderabad and the Maratha Empire, and Tipu Sultan, the ruler of Mysore.

### Battle of Seringapatam, 1799

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Battle of Seringapatam, 1799")]

Main article: [Siege of Seringapatam (1799)](/wiki/Siege_of_Seringapatam_(1799) "Siege of Seringapatam (1799)")

Srirangapatna was the scene of the last and decisive battle of the [Fourth Anglo-Mysore War](/wiki/Fourth_Anglo-Mysore_War "Fourth Anglo-Mysore War"), fought in 1799 between [Tipu Sultan](/wiki/Tipu_Sultan "Tipu Sultan"), ruler of [Mysore](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mysore "Kingdom of Mysore"), and a combined force of 50,000 men provided equally by the [Nizam of Hyderabad](/wiki/Nizam_of_Hyderabad "Nizam of Hyderabad") and the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company"), under the overall command of [General George Harris](/wiki/George_Harris,_1st_Baron_Harris "George Harris, 1st Baron Harris").

At the battle's climax, Tipu Sultan was killed within the fort of Seringapatam, betrayed by one of his own confidants. The spot where he fell is today marked by a memorial. Although the joint forces of the victorious army plundered Seringapatam and ransacked Tipu's palace, much of the site of the battle is still intact, including the ramparts, the Water Gate, the area where British prisoners were held, and the site of the destroyed palace.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Apart from the usual gold and cash, innumerable other valuables were shipped to England, including *objets d'art* and personal possessions of Tipu Sultan such as his rich clothing, shoes, sword, and firearms. Most of these remain in the British [Royal Collection](/wiki/Royal_Collection "Royal Collection") and in the [Victoria and Albert Museum](/wiki/Victoria_and_Albert_Museum "Victoria and Albert Museum"), the latter of which holds [Tipu's Tiger](/wiki/Tipu%27s_Tiger "Tipu's Tiger"), an [automaton](/wiki/Automaton "Automaton") of a tiger on top of a British soldier. The sword of Tipu Sultan was purchased at a [Sotheby's](/wiki/Sotheby%27s "Sotheby's") auction by [Vijay Mallya](/wiki/Vijay_Mallya "Vijay Mallya"), a liquor baron from [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka").

British composer [Harriet Wainwright](/wiki/Harriet_Wainwright "Harriet Wainwright") composed a work for chorus ("Seringapatam") about the battle.

## Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Location")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Seringapatam_plan.jpg/250px-Seringapatam_plan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Seringapatam_plan.jpg)

Map

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Kaveri_View_Rail_Bridge_Srirangapatna_Jul22_A7C_02120.jpg/250px-Kaveri_View_Rail_Bridge_Srirangapatna_Jul22_A7C_02120.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kaveri_View_Rail_Bridge_Srirangapatna_Jul22_A7C_02120.jpg)

Kaveri down to the western rail bridge

Although situated only 15 km (9.3 mi) from [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore") city, Srirangapatna lies in the neighbouring district of [Mandya](/wiki/Mandya_District "Mandya District"). The entire town is enclosed by the river [Kaveri](/wiki/Kaveri "Kaveri") to form a [river island](/wiki/River_island "River island"), the northern half of which is shown in the adjacent image. While the main river flows on the eastern side of the island, the *Paschima Vaahini* segment of the same river flows to its west. The town is easily accessible by train from [Bengaluru](/wiki/Bengaluru "Bengaluru") and Mysore and is also well-connected by road, lying as it does just off the Bangalore-Mysore [National Highway 275](/wiki/National_Highway_275_(India) "National Highway 275 (India)"). The highway passes through this town and special care was taken to minimize any impact on the monuments.

## Religious significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Religious significance")]

The town takes its name from the celebrated [Ranganathaswamy temple](/wiki/Sri_Ranganathaswamy_Temple_(Srirangapatna) "Sri Ranganathaswamy Temple (Srirangapatna)") which dominates the town, making Srirangapatna one of the most important [Vaishnavite](/wiki/Vaishnavism "Vaishnavism") centers of pilgrimage in [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India"). The temple was built by the [Ganga dynasty](/wiki/Western_Ganga_Dynasty "Western Ganga Dynasty") rulers of the area in the 9th century; the structure was strengthened and improved upon architecturally some three centuries later. Thus, the temple is a medley of the [Hoysala](/wiki/Hoysala_architecture "Hoysala architecture") and [Vijayanagar](/wiki/Vijayanagara_Empire "Vijayanagara Empire") styles of [temple architecture](/wiki/Hindu_temple_architecture "Hindu temple architecture").

Tradition holds that all the islands formed in the [Kaveri River](/wiki/Kaveri_River "Kaveri River") are consecrated to Sri Ranganathaswamy ([PrabhaVishnu](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu") mentioned in [Vishnu Sahasranama](/wiki/Vishnu_Sahasranama "Vishnu Sahasranama")) and large temples have been built in very ancient times dedicated to that deity on the three largest islands. The Srirangapatna temple is one of the three temples of the God [Ranganatha](/wiki/Ranganatha "Ranganatha") (Adi Ranga) that are situated in the natural islands formed in the Kaveri river. They are:

* Adi Ranga: the [Sri Ranganathaswamy Temple](/wiki/Sri_Ranganathaswamy_Temple,_Srirangapatna "Sri Ranganathaswamy Temple, Srirangapatna") at Srirangapatna, Srirangapatna taluk, [Mandya district](/wiki/Mandya_district "Mandya district"), [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"), [India](/wiki/India "India")
* Madhya Ranga: the [Sri Ranganathaswamy Temple](/wiki/Sri_Ranganathaswamy_Temple_(Shivanasamudra) "Sri Ranganathaswamy Temple (Shivanasamudra)") at [Shivanasamudra](/wiki/Shivanasamudra "Shivanasamudra"), [Kollegala taluk](/wiki/Kollegal "Kollegal"), [Chamarajanagara district](/wiki/Chamrajnagar_district "Chamrajnagar district"), [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"), [India](/wiki/India "India")
* Antya Ranga: the [Sri Ranganathaswamy Temple](/wiki/Ranganathaswamy_Temple,_Srirangam "Ranganathaswamy Temple, Srirangam") at [Srirangam](/wiki/Srirangam "Srirangam"), [Srirangam taluk](/wiki/Srirangam_taluk "Srirangam taluk"), [Tiruchirappalli district](/wiki/Tiruchirappalli_district "Tiruchirappalli district"), [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu"), [India](/wiki/India "India")

The presence of the Kaveri River is in itself considered auspicious and sanctifying. The *Paschima Vaahini* section of the Kaveri at Srirangapatna is considered especially sacred; the pious come from far and wide to immerse the ashes of the departed and perform obsequies to their ancestors in these waters.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Demographics")]

According to the 2011 India Census Data, Srirangapatna had a population of 155,130 persons. Males constitute 50.06% and females constitute 49.93% of the total population. 9.80% of the population are under 6 years of age.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

Srirangapatna is at [12°25′N 76°42′E﻿ / ﻿12.41°N 76.7°E﻿ / 12.41; 76.7](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Srirangapatna&params=12.41_N_76.7_E_). It has an average elevation of 679 metres (2,228 ft) above mean sea-level.

## Places of interest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Places of interest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Sriragapatna_Elephant_gate.jpg/250px-Sriragapatna_Elephant_gate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sriragapatna_Elephant_gate.jpg)

Vegetable market at Elephant Gate

The town is famous for a very ancient temple dedicated to Sri [Ranganathaswamy](/wiki/Ranganatha "Ranganatha"), a form of Lord [Vishnu](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu"). There is also Kalyani Siddhi Vinayaka Temple in front of the Sri Ranganathaswamy Temple. Other temples in Srirangapatna include the Lakshminarasimha Swamy Temple, Jyothi Mahaswara Temple, Bidhcotta Ganesha Temple, Panduranga Swamy Temple, the Sathyanarayana Swamy Temple, the Anjunaya Swamy Temple, the Ayyapa Temple, the Gangadhareswara Swamy Temple, and RaganathaNagara Ganesha Temple, Lakshmi Temple, Sri Raghavendra Swamy Mutt on Old Post Office Road, surrounding Srirangapatna in fort 8 Ganesh & Anjunaya temples. The [Karighatta](/wiki/Karighatta "Karighatta") (Black Hill) and its temple of Lord [Srinivasa](/wiki/Venkateshwara "Venkateshwara") is a few kilometres from the town. The deity is Kari-giri-vasa (one who resides on the black hill). The renowned [Nimishambha](/wiki/Nimishambha "Nimishambha") Temple is about 2 km from the town. Srirangapatna also hosts the summer palace of Tipu Sultan and his mausoleum.

The Ranganthaswamy Temple – usually referred to as "Sri Ranganathaswamy" – is dedicated to [Ranganatha](/wiki/Ranganatha "Ranganatha"), a manifestation of [Vishnu](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu"). It is one of the five important pilgrimage sites along the river Kaveri for devotees of Ranganatha. These five sacred sites are together known as *Pancharanga Kshetrams* in Southern India. Since Srirangapatna is the first temple starting from upstream, the deity is known as Adi Ranga (lit; "first Ranga"), and the town of Srirangapatna, which derives its name from the temple, is on an island in the river Kaveri.

### Daria Daulat Bagh

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Daria Daulat Bagh")]

Main article: [Daria Daulat Bagh](/wiki/Daria_Daulat_Bagh "Daria Daulat Bagh")

* [![Palace of Tipu Sultan](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Daria_daulat.jpg/500px-Daria_daulat.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daria_daulat.jpg "Palace of Tipu Sultan")

The *Dariya Daulat Palace* (Summer Palace) is set amidst beautiful gardens called Daria Daulat Bagh. Tippu Sultan built this palace in 1784. The palace is built in the Indo-[Saracenic](/wiki/Saracen "Saracen") style is mostly made of teakwood. The palace has a rectangular plan and is built on a raised platform.

Other attractions in Srirangapatna include the *[Jama Masjid](/wiki/Masjid-i-Ala "Masjid-i-Ala")* (a [Mosque](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque")) and the Dariya Daulat Gardens. The mosque has stone Arabic inscriptions which mention the 99 titles given to the almighty Allah, and the [Farsi](/wiki/Farsi "Farsi") inscription which mentions that the Masjid was built in AD 1782 by Tipu Sultan.[[9]](#cite_note-Ch-DHarya-9)

### Tipu Sultan Gumbaz

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Tipu Sultan Gumbaz")]

Main article: [Gumbaz, Seringapatam](/wiki/Gumbaz,_Seringapatam "Gumbaz, Seringapatam")

The *Gumbaz* is an impeccably detailed mausoleum and houses the remains of Tipu Sultan, his father Hyder Ali and his mother Fatima Begum among beautifully manicured gardens. Various tombs of other relatives surround the *gumbaz*, some with small signs offering guidance on which specific individuals are buried here. The outer *gumbaz* columns are made of [amphibolite](/wiki/Amphibolite "Amphibolite"), a very dark rock that exudes a somber richness. Handcrafted door frames covered in a deep lacquer finish lead into an inner tomb illuminated only by natural light. All visitors are welcome inside, and even encouraged to enter by the friendly doormen.

### Wellesley Bridge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Wellesley Bridge")]

[Wellesley Bridge](/wiki/Wellesley_Bridge,_Srirangapatna "Wellesley Bridge, Srirangapatna") was erected by Kingdom of Mysore's [Dewan](/wiki/Dewan "Dewan"), [Purnaiah](/wiki/Purnaiah "Purnaiah") on the Kaveri river in 1804.[[10]](#cite_note-10) It was named after the then Governor General Marquis of Wellesley. The bridge is built of stone pillars and stone corbels and surrounded by stone girders. The bridge is very strong and has survived the heavy traffic of many years.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Karighatta viewpoint

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Karighatta viewpoint")]

The [Karighatta](/wiki/Karighatta_temple "Karighatta temple") viewpoint gives good panorama of Mysore and Srirangapatna cities from a height of 3,000 feet. Karighatta is a hill a few kilometres outside the 'island' town of Srirangapatna. The name Karighatta translates to "Black Hill" in [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada"). The hill supports the dry scrub jungle and many [tamarind](/wiki/Tamarind "Tamarind") and [gooseberry](/wiki/Gooseberry "Gooseberry") trees are found around the temple. A small river, Lokapavani, a tributary of [Kaveri](/wiki/Kaveri "Kaveri") flows by the hill. The main entrance to the temple, with huge wooden doors opens into a large quadrangle, which is the main shrine for Vaikunta [Srinivasa](/wiki/Srinivasa "Srinivasa") in black stone, flanked by Yoga [Srinivasa](/wiki/Srinivasa "Srinivasa") (without his consort) and Bhoga [Srinivasa](/wiki/Srinivasa "Srinivasa") idols. The hill may be climbed by stone steps (450 in number). A winding paved road is used for vehicular transport. The stone steps lead to a flat hilltop where the temple stands. The hillock has a superb panoramic view of Srirangapatna and [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore"). The beautiful landscape around the hill and the confluence of the [Kaveri](/wiki/Kaveri "Kaveri") and Lokapavani rivers can be seen from atop the hill.

### Nimishamba Temple

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Nimishamba Temple")]

The famous [Nimishamba](/wiki/Nimishamba "Nimishamba") (the incarnation of [Parvathi](/wiki/Parvathi "Parvathi"), the goddess wife of Lord [Shiva](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva")) temple is on the bank of the Lokapavani river. This temple too can be clearly seen from the top of the [Karighatta](/wiki/Karighatta "Karighatta") hill. It is a belief that [Parvathi](/wiki/Parvathi "Parvathi") will clear all the problems and troubles of her devotees within a minute (*nimisha* in [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit")), and hence the name.

### Garrison Cemetery, Srirangapatna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Garrison Cemetery, Srirangapatna")]

Main article: [Garrison Cemetery, Srirangapatna](/wiki/Garrison_Cemetery,_Srirangapatna "Garrison Cemetery, Srirangapatna")

The Garrison Cemetery is located in Srirangapatna, on the banks of the river [Cauvery](/wiki/Cauvery "Cauvery"), about 300m from the Bangalore Mysore Highway. It has about 307 graves of the European officers killed in the [final assault](/wiki/Siege_of_Srirangapatna_(1799) "Siege of Srirangapatna (1799)") on [Tippu Sultan](/wiki/Tippu_Sultan "Tippu Sultan") in 1799, and their family members. Among the graves, there are 80 graves of the officers of the Swiss [Regiment de Meuron](/wiki/Regiment_de_Meuron "Regiment de Meuron"), and the rest of the graves are their family members.[[13]](#cite_note-Hindu-Garrision-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Mysore-14)

### Scott’s Bungalow, Seringapatam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Scott’s Bungalow, Seringapatam")]

Main article: [Scott’s Bungalow, Seringapatam](/wiki/Scott%E2%80%99s_Bungalow,_Seringapatam "Scott’s Bungalow, Seringapatam")

The Scott’s Bungalow is located in [Seringapatam](/wiki/Seringapatam "Seringapatam") on the banks of the river [Cauvery](/wiki/Cauvery "Cauvery"), at about half a mile from the Mysore Gate of the Seringapatam gate.[[15]](#cite_note-Chetti-15) The bungalow was the residence of Col. Scott, an officer of the [Madras Army](/wiki/Madras_Army "Madras Army") who took part in the [siege of Seringapatam](/wiki/Siege_of_Seringapatam_(1799) "Siege of Seringapatam (1799)") in 1799. The bungalow is associated with the legend and tragedy of Col. Scott. The story of Scott’s Bungalow is lamented in a poem by Walter Yeldham called *The Deserted Bungalow*, published in 1875.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Harshitha-17)

### Lord Harris's House

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Lord Harris's House")]

Between the Garrison Cemetery and [Scott’s Bungalow](/wiki/Scott%E2%80%99s_Bungalow,_Seringapatam "Scott’s Bungalow, Seringapatam") a path leads to Lord Harris's House on the river banks. This house is known as *Lord Harris's House* or *The Doctor's Bungalow* or *Puraniah's Bungalow*. This house was the residence of General Harris, for a short time after the [siege of Seringapatam](/wiki/Siege_of_Seringapatam_(1799) "Siege of Seringapatam (1799)") in 1799, and went on to become headquarters of the commanding officer of Seringapatam. In 1809, the house was the scene of a mutiny by officers of the [Madras Army](/wiki/Madras_Army "Madras Army"), led by Col. Bell, against Sir. George Barlow, the Governor of Madras. [Purnaiah](/wiki/Purnaiah "Purnaiah"), lived in this house after his retirement from service in 1811, and died there on 28 March 1812. A tablet on the wall records the connection of Lord Harris and Puraniah to the house.[[18]](#cite_note-Parsons-18)

### Srirangapatna Fort

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Srirangapatna Fort")]

Main article: [Srirangapatna Fort](/wiki/Srirangapatna_Fort "Srirangapatna Fort")

The fortress is in the west of the island, and is surrounded by double walls. The point at which the British broke through the walls, and thus Tipu Sultan's troops surprised, is marked by an obelisk. Also highlighted is the place where Tipu Sultan was killed by the British forces. In addition, the dungeon in which the Mysore rulers had imprisoned British soldiers.

### Colonel Bailey's Dungeon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Colonel Bailey's Dungeon")]

Main article: [Colonel Bailey's Dungeon](/wiki/Colonel_Bailey%27s_Dungeon "Colonel Bailey's Dungeon")

[Colonel Bailey's Dungeon](/wiki/Colonel_Bailey%27s_Dungeon "Colonel Bailey's Dungeon") was the place where Tipu Sultan used to imprison all the British officers. It is near the death place memorial of Tipu Sultan and is surrounded by gardens on all four sides. Bailey was the only British officer who died in that place and could not make it through the tough conditions and so it was later named after him.

Another version says that the commanding officer of the British at the [Battle of Pollilur (1780)](/wiki/Battle_of_Pollilur_(1780) "Battle of Pollilur (1780)"), Col. Baillie, was imprisoned here after the defeat of his troops in the [First Mysore War](/wiki/First_Mysore_War "First Mysore War").

Obelisk Monument

### Obelisk Monument

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Obelisk Monument")]

Main article: [Obelisk Monument](/wiki/Obelisk_Monument "Obelisk Monument")

[Obelisk in Srirangapatna](/wiki/Obelisk_Monument "Obelisk Monument") is a memorial erected by the then government of Mysore in memory of the English soldiers died during the siege of Srirangapatna. The Memorial was built during the reign of [Krishnaraja Wadiyar IV](/wiki/Krishnaraja_Wadiyar_IV "Krishnaraja Wadiyar IV") in 1907, to celebrate the defeat of Tipu in 1799. The inscription on the plaque reads "This monument is erected by the government of Mysore in 1907 in order to commemorate the siege of Seringapatam by the British forces under lieutenant general G. Harris ([George Harris, 1st Baron Harris](/wiki/George_Harris,_1st_Baron_Harris "George Harris, 1st Baron Harris")) and its final capture by assault on 4 May 1799. As also the names of those gallant officers who fell during the operations."

### Ranganathittu Bird Sanctuary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Ranganathittu Bird Sanctuary")]

Main article: [Ranganathittu Bird Sanctuary](/wiki/Ranganathittu_Bird_Sanctuary "Ranganathittu Bird Sanctuary")

Near the town is the [Ranganathittu Bird Sanctuary](/wiki/Ranganathittu_Bird_Sanctuary "Ranganathittu Bird Sanctuary"), where several [bird](/wiki/Bird "Bird") species breed, including the [painted stork](/wiki/Painted_stork "Painted stork"), [open-billed stork](/wiki/Asian_openbill_stork "Asian openbill stork"), [black-headed ibis](/wiki/Black-headed_ibis "Black-headed ibis"), [river tern](/wiki/River_tern_(bird) "River tern (bird)"), [great stone plover](/wiki/Great_stone_plover "Great stone plover") and [Indian shag](/wiki/Indian_shag "Indian shag").

## In literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Srirangapatna&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: In literature")]

* [Bernard Cornwell](/wiki/Bernard_Cornwell "Bernard Cornwell")'s *[Sharpe's Tiger](/wiki/Sharpe%27s_Tiger "Sharpe's Tiger")*[[19]](#cite_note-19) is a fictionalised account of the Battle of Seringapatam. It concentrates on the exploits of the fictional character of Richard Sharpe, and the historic Arthur Wellesley, later the [Duke of Wellington](/wiki/Duke_of_Wellington "Duke of Wellington").
* [Wilkie Collins](/wiki/Wilkie_Collins "Wilkie Collins")' *[The Moonstone](/wiki/The_Moonstone "The Moonstone")*[[20]](#cite_note-20) includes a prologue that takes place during the Battle of Srirangapatna, entitled "The Storming of Seringapatam (1799)", during which a British officer steals a sacred Hindu diamond that becomes the mystery at the centre of the novel.
* [John Forster](/wiki/John_Forster_(biographer) "John Forster (biographer)") mentions in *The Life of Charles Dickens* that in a childhood playground of C.D., "he had been... delivered from the dungeons of Seringapatam, an immense pile ('of haycock'), by the victorious British ('boy next door and his two cousins')...". See p. 10 of the Everyman edition published in 1969. First published: London; Chapman and Hall, 1872–1874, in 3 volumes.
